metadata.title: Logging Monarch Butterflies for Citizen Science
metadata.curiosityNote: Monarch surveys count within flagged areas, so butterflies that leave the perimeter drop out of the tally.

story.briefTemplate: Along a prairie trail Seraphina and her class helped count monarch butterflies in a citizen science project helping nonprofit Monarch Joint Venture. In the chilly morning, the team spread out and counted {quantity:area_one_monarchs} resting with closed wings in area one. They hiked to the other side of a lake, and with the warm sun out the monarchs took flight to feed on milkweed. With help from a video camera on a tripod and AI analysis of a flagged-in perimeter, the team spotted {quantity:area_two_monarchs} in area two. During logging, a group of noisy hikers came through blasting music. The noise and vibrations caused {quantity:monarchs_scattered} to fly out of the prairie toward an adjacent shopping center.
story.causalEvent: Noisy hikers caused some monarchs to fly out of the flagged prairie area.
story.closingNoteTemplate: The butterfly log closed with a best known estimate of {quantity:monarchs_remaining_estimate} in the prairie.

label.child: Monarchs resting in the first area
Monarchs feeding in the second area
Best estimate still in the prairie

steps.prompt: How many monarchs did the team spot in total before the hikers came through?
steps.reasoningPrompt: The team counted monarchs in area one and area two. How do those two counts make the total?

backwardCheck.prompt: Take the area one count back out. Do you land on the area two count?

steps.prompt: After some monarchs scattered beyond the counting flags, what is the best known estimate of how many were still in the prairie?
steps.reasoningPrompt: Some monarchs left the flagged prairie area. Did the best known estimate go up or down?

backwardCheck.prompt: Put the scattered monarchs back in. Do you return to the count before the hikers?

operatorExperiments.alternateWorldTemplate: This matches the story: {label:area_one_monarchs.lowercase} and {label:area_two_monarchs.lowercase} combine into all the monarchs before the hikers.
Subtracting would fit a different story where {quantity:area_two_monarchs} flew off instead of joining the count.
This would fit a different question: if there were {value:area_two_monarchs} groups like {label:area_one_monarchs.lowercase}, how many monarchs would there be?
This would fit a different question about sharing {label:area_one_monarchs.lowercase} into equal groups.
This matches the story: the monarchs that scattered left the prairie, lowering the estimate.
Adding would fit a different story where {quantity:monarchs_scattered} flew in instead of scattering away.
This would fit a different question: if there were {value:monarchs_scattered} groups like {label:monarchs_before_hikers.lowercase}, how many monarchs would there be?
This would fit a different question about sharing {label:monarchs_before_hikers.lowercase} into equal groups.

recap.headline: Why the prairie estimate became {quantity:monarchs_remaining_estimate}
causalChain: The class counted {quantity:area_one_monarchs} in area one.
The camera and AI analysis found {quantity:area_two_monarchs} in area two.
After {quantity:monarchs_scattered} scattered away, the best known estimate was {quantity:monarchs_remaining_estimate}.

dataQuestion.prompt: What does {quantity:monarchs_scattered} represent in the prairie model?
dataQuestion.correctFeedback: Right. {quantity:monarchs_scattered} is the number that left the flagged prairie area.
dataQuestion.incorrectFeedback: That amount is the number that left the flagged prairie area: {quantity:monarchs_scattered}.
